1: The first day of the seventh month is a day of trumpet blasts (yom teruˁah) — a holy convocation and no ordinary work. The seventh month is the most sacred month of the Hebrew calendar, containing the three autumn feasts (Trumpets, Atonement, Tabernacles). The trumpet blasts (teruˁah) are a signal of divine encounter — the same blast as at Sinai (Exodus 19:16) and in the eschatological texts: 'in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet' (1 Corinthians 15:52); 'the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God' (1 Thessalonians 4:16).
2: The Trumpets offering: one young bull, one ram, seven male lambs — a slightly scaled-down version of the Passover/Weeks offering (those had two bulls). The reduced scale for Trumpets (one bull vs. two) reflects the graduated hierarchy of the feast calendar: the New Year's trumpet-day is honored but distinguished from the full-scale Passover offering. The feast hierarchy maps the theological weight of the events: Passover (redemption) > Trumpets (call to assembly/repentance).
3: The grain offerings accompanying the Trumpets burnt offering in standard proportions. The feast of Trumpets was traditionally understood in later Judaism as the New Year (Rosh Hashanah) and as a time of divine judgment — YHWH opening the books. The association of trumpets with divine judgment and the announcement of kingship connects to Rev 8–11 (the seven trumpet angels) and Revelation 11:15: 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ.'
4: The male goat sin offering for Trumpets. Atonement precedes the trumpet blast — the call to assembly is a call to atonement. In the later Jewish tradition, the Trumpets feast (Rosh Hashanah) begins the Ten Days of Awe that culminate in the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur). The liturgical sequence — trumpet call → ten-day repentance → atonement — is the covenant logic of approach: you cannot enter YHWH's presence without atonement.
5: One male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular monthly and daily offerings. The sin offering at Trumpets layers on the sin offerings of the month (new moon) and the day (daily tamid). The cumulative sin-offering structure across the calendar communicates the pervasiveness of sin and the pervasiveness of atonement: every day, every month, every feast requires atoning sacrifice. Hebrews 10:3 notes that this repetition itself was 'a reminder of sins every year.'
6: The Trumpets offerings are in addition to the regular monthly and daily burnt offerings. The additive structure places Trumpets within the whole offering system, not as a replacement but as a fulfillment. Every level of the system is active simultaneously: daily, monthly, and annually. The NT equivalent is the cumulative work of Christ: once-for-all sacrifice (annual), ongoing intercession (daily), and the final trumpet that will announce the kingdom's consummation.
7: The tenth day of the seventh month is the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) — a holy convocation, affliction of souls (ˁinui nefesh, fasting), and no work. Yom Kippur is the culminating day of the Israelite year, the one day when the high priest enters the holy of holies (Lev 16). Hebrews devotes three chapters (7–10) to the typological significance of Yom Kippur, concluding: 'But when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come, then through the greater and more perfect tent (not made with hands, that is, not of this creation) he entered once for all into the holy places... securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:11–12).
8: The Yom Kippur burnt offering: one young bull, one ram, seven male lambs — the same as Trumpets. The Day of Atonement's burnt offering is identical in scale to the Trumpets offering, reflecting the coherence of the autumn feast season as a single theological movement: Trumpets (call to assembly and repentance) → Atonement (the atoning sacrifice itself) → Tabernacles (the joyful dwelling in the presence of the atoned God). The three autumn feasts are the gospel arc compressed into one month.
9: The grain offerings for Yom Kippur. The grain offering's presence on Yom Kippur — alongside the burnt offering — maintains the meal-structure of covenant communion even on the most solemn day. Atonement and fellowship are not opposites: atonement is the foundation of fellowship. The Day of Atonement is ultimately a day of reconciliation and communion restored, not merely a day of penalty. 'God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them' (2 Corinthians 5:19).
10: One male goat as a sin offering for Yom Kippur, in addition to the special Yom Kippur sin offerings of Lev 16. The sin offering listed here is the communal burnt-offering sin-offering; Lev 16 describes the unique scapegoat and high-priestly offerings. The two accounts together give the full Yom Kippur picture: communal burning offerings (Num 29) and the unique atoning/scapegoat ritual (Lev 16). Christ's atonement encompasses both: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24) — the scapegoat; and 'He entered once for all into the holy places... by means of his own blood' (Hebrews 9:12) — the high-priestly offering.
11: The Yom Kippur sin offering is in addition to the atonement sin offering (Lev 16) and the regular offerings. The layering of atonement offerings on Yom Kippur is maximal — the day of maximum sacrifice. Hebrews 10:1–4 comments on this maximum: 'it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.' The maximum of the OT system still falls infinitely short of actual sin-removal. Only Christ's blood actually removes sin: 'the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin' (1 John 1:7).
12: The fifteenth day of the seventh month is the feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) — a holy convocation for seven days. Tabernacles is the harvest festival of booths, commemorating Israel's wilderness dwelling in tents. Zechariah 14:16–19 prophesies that in the eschatological age all nations will come to Jerusalem to celebrate Tabernacles. John 1:14 uses the Tabernacles/booth language: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us' — the incarnation is the ultimate Tabernacles fulfillment.
13: Day one of Tabernacles: thirteen bulls — the largest single-day offering in the entire festival calendar. The Tabernacles offering begins at its maximum (13 bulls) and counts down by one each day until day seven (7 bulls), totaling 70 bulls over the seven days. The seventy bulls are traditionally associated with the seventy nations of the Table of Nations (Gen 10) — Israel's seven-day Tabernacles sacrifice intercedes for all the world's nations. The universalism of Christ's atonement ('for the sins of the whole world,' 1 John 2:2) is the antitype of this universal sacrifice.
14: The grain offerings accompanying the first-day Tabernacles bulls and rams. Thirteen bulls × three-tenths of an ephah = thirty-nine-tenths of an ephah of fine flour for the grain offering. The total grain offerings across all seven days of Tabernacles are enormous — the feast of harvest is the feast of abundance, and the offerings reflect the land's bounty. The harvest abundance of Tabernacles points to the eschatological harvest: 'Blessed are you who are hungry now, for you shall be satisfied' (Luke 6:21).
15: Fourteen male lambs on day one of Tabernacles (two per day through the seven days). The lambs remain constant at fourteen (two per day) even as the bulls count down. The stability of the lamb-number amid the declining bull-count is a structural feature of the Tabernacles calendar: the lamb is the constant, foundational element. 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29) — the Lamb is the permanent center amid all the variation of the festival calendar's offerings.
16: One male goat as sin offering for day one of Tabernacles. The sin offering appears every day throughout Tabernacles (Numbers 29:16, 19, 22, 25, 28, 31, 34) — seven sin offerings across seven days. Atonement is the daily foundation of even the most joyful feast in the calendar. The connection of joy and atonement is the gospel's own structure: 'In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace, which he lavished upon us' (Ephesians 1:7–8). Lavish grace requires lavish atonement.
17: Day two of Tabernacles: twelve bulls (one fewer than day one). The descending count begins: 13, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 7 — seven days, seventy bulls total. The countdown toward Tabernacles' final day is a kind of liturgical crescendo-in-reverse: the feast starts high and counts toward its end. The counting-down is itself a theological statement: the feast is finite, earthly, and points beyond itself to the day when the feast is permanently fulfilled and needs no annual repetition.
18: The grain and drink offerings accompanying day two's bulls. The accompanying offerings remain consistent through the Tabernacles countdown. The consistency of the grain and drink offerings even as the bulls decline creates the sense of a sustaining constant beneath the changing count — the covenant meal (grain + drink) remains intact even as the sacrificial scale shifts. Christ is 'the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8) — the constant beneath all covenant variation.
19: One male goat sin offering on day two. The sin offering's daily recurrence through Tabernacles is the structural reminder that even within the most joyful feast, the community requires daily atonement. The repetition encodes the principle that Paul applies to the church: 'Examine yourselves, to see whether you are in the faith' (2 Corinthians 13:5). The feast does not suspend self-examination; it requires it.
20: Day three: eleven bulls. The countdown continues. The reduction by one bull each day through seven days creates a liturgical clock that marks the feast's progression. The feast has a structure and a duration — it is not indefinite. The eschatological feast will have no end: 'You will fill me with joy in your presence, with eternal pleasures at your right hand' (Psalms 16:11) — the earthly Tabernacles feast is finite and counted; the heavenly feast is endless and uncounted.
21: Grain offerings for day three. The sustained grain-offering through seven days of Tabernacles creates the sense of a week-long shared meal with YHWH. The meal character of Tabernacles is explicit in its origins: Israel eating in booths, sharing the harvest. Jesus' wilderness feeding of the five thousand occurs near Passover (John 6:4) in the context of Tabernacles imagery — the crowds in the wilderness eating miraculous bread, like the wilderness generation eating manna during the years of Tabernacles-style living.
22: One male goat sin offering on day three. The sin offerings through Tabernacles create a sustained awareness that the feast's joy rests on atonement. The joy of the harvest feast is not a naive joy that ignores sin but a grateful joy that knows it has been atoned for. 'Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered' (Psalms 32:1) — the blessedness of forgiveness is the foundation of the feast's joy.
23: Day four: ten bulls. The midpoint of the seven-day countdown. Ten is the number of completion (the ten commandments, the ten plagues) — at day four (ten bulls), the feast is halfway through its liturgical clock. The mathematical precision of the Tabernacles offering calendar reflects the same precision as the census arithmetic: YHWH's worship is ordered, not random.
24: Grain offerings for day four. The total grain offerings through four days of Tabernacles are enormous. The harvest feast's grain offerings reflect the agricultural abundance that YHWH's blessing on the land produced. 'The earth is the Lord's, and the fullness thereof' (Psalms 24:1) — the harvest offerings are the formal acknowledgment that the land's abundance is a divine gift, not a human achievement.
25: One male goat sin offering on day four. The sin offering at the feast's midpoint is the theological center: even at the harvest's peak, atonement is required. The NT principle: 'If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us. If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness' (1 John 1:8–9). The daily sin offering throughout the feast is the OT form of this daily confession and cleansing.
26: Day five: nine bulls. The countdown approaches its end — 13, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 7. Nine is significant in OT numerology (nine fruits of the Spirit, Galatians 5:22–23; nine listed beatitudes in some traditions), though the primary significance here is structural: the fifth day's nine bulls is simply part of the decreasing sequence. The sequence creates anticipation: what happens at the end of the countdown?
27: Grain and drink offerings for day five. The sustained offering across five days of Tabernacles creates a week of sustained worship intensity unmatched anywhere in the OT calendar. Tabernacles is, by offering-count alone, the most worship-intensive week of the year. The eschatological feast of Revelation 7:9–17 (the great multitude before the throne) is the consummation of this worship intensity: 'Therefore they are before the throne of God, and serve him day and night in his temple.'
28: One male goat sin offering on day five. The sin offering on day five is the same as on every other day: one goat. The constancy of the sin offering amid the variation of the burnt offering count communicates the constancy of the need for atonement regardless of calendar position. Christ's atonement is similarly constant and unchanging: 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8) — the same effective sacrifice regardless of when one comes to it.
29: Day six: eight bulls. The penultimate day before the great seven-bull offering. Eight in OT theology is associated with new beginning (circumcision on the eighth day; the eighth day after the Sabbath = resurrection). The eighth day after Tabernacles begins (the day after the seventh-day closing feast) becomes the 'Shemini Atzeret' — the solemn assembly that closes the feast season. The new-beginning theology of eight surrounds the final days of the feast.
30: Grain and drink offerings for day six. The feast is nearing its end; the offerings continue without reduction except in the bull count. The persistence of the full grain and drink offerings even as the bulls decline communicates the principle that the covenant meal (sustenance and fellowship) does not diminish even as the sacrificial apparatus approaches its terminus. The meal continues; what changes is the scale of the offering.
31: One male goat sin offering on day six. The sixth sin offering in six days of Tabernacles. The cumulative weight of seven daily sin offerings through the feast encodes the thoroughness of the covenant's atonement: not one day of the feast passes without sacrifice for sin. Paul: 'I die every day!' (1 Corinthians 15:31) — the apostle's daily dying mirrors the daily sin-offering of the feast calendar, the daily recapitulation of the cross in the believer's life.
32: Day seven of Tabernacles: seven bulls. The great day of the feast ends with seven — the number of completion and covenant. The seven-day feast's final day with seven bulls closes the countdown at the most theologically resonant number. John 7:37 places Christ's great cry — 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink' — on 'the last day of the feast, the great day,' which is this seventh day of Tabernacles. The feast's climax becomes the moment of Christ's greatest self-revelation.
33: Grain and drink offerings for the seventh day. The final day's grain and drink offerings complete the week-long meal-offering. The feast ends as it began: with grain, drink, and the smell of sacrifice. The completeness of the offering on day seven mirrors the completeness of Christ's work: 'It is finished' (tetelestai, John 19:30) — the Greek perfect tense indicating a completed action with ongoing effects. The feast is finished; the atonement it pointed to is permanently accomplished.
34: On the eighth day, a solemn assembly (ˁatzeret) — a holy convocation and no regular work. The eighth-day assembly closes the feast season. In Jewish tradition this becomes Shemini Atzeret and Simchat Torah (rejoicing in the Torah). The eighth day — after the seven-day feast — carries the new-beginning symbolism: seven days of earthly feast, then the eighth day that points beyond the cycle. The resurrection of Christ on 'the first day of the week' (which is also the eighth day after Palm Sunday's entry into Jerusalem) is the great Eighth Day: the new creation beginning after the old creation's week is complete.
35: The eighth-day offering: one bull, one ram, seven lambs — the most scaled-down offering of the entire feast season. The climax of the calendar ends quietly: from thirteen bulls on day one to one bull on day eight, from maximum sacrifice to minimum. The downward movement is not a diminishment but a resolution: the feast has spent itself completely, pouring out its maximum at the beginning and tapering to its quiet close. The cross is the maximum; Christ's ongoing intercession is the quiet, persistent continuation.
36: The grain offerings for the eighth day in standard proportions. The eighth-day grain offering accompanies the single bull as usual — the meal-structure is maintained even at the feast's quietest moment. The covenant meal does not disappear when the great feast ends; it continues in the daily, monthly, and annual rhythm. In Christ, the covenant meal continues: 'as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26).
37: One male goat sin offering on the eighth day. The feast season closes, as it began, with atonement. The final sin offering of the Tabernacles season places the seal of atonement on the entire festival calendar: every feast begins and ends with the acknowledgment that covenant communion requires the covering of sin. Christ's work bookends the covenant in the same way: 'the Lamb who was slain from the foundation of the world' (Revelation 13:8) — atonement is not an incident in redemptive history but its permanent foundation.
38: The eighth-day offerings are in addition to the regular burnt offering and grain and drink offering. The additive structure persists to the very last verse of the festival calendar. The tamid (daily offering) underlies and continues even on the feast's final day — the regular rhythm of daily worship is never suspended by the extraordinary rhythm of the annual feasts. Christ's perpetual intercession (the tamid) underlies all the extraordinary acts of redemptive history.
39: 'These you shall offer to YHWH at your appointed feasts, in addition to your vow offerings and your freewill offerings, for your burnt offerings, and for your grain offerings, and for your drink offerings, and for your peace offerings.' The closing summary names the mandatory festival calendar (moˁadim) alongside the optional offerings (vows and freewill). The mandatory system provides the covenant structure; the optional offerings provide space for personal devotion. The NT equivalent: the Lord's Supper is the mandatory covenant meal; personal prayer and fasting are the freewill offerings beyond it.
40: Moses tells the Israelites everything that YHWH commanded him. The compliance formula closes the two-chapter festival calendar with Moses' complete obedience in transmission. The entire offering calendar — daily, Sabbath, monthly, annual, with all their grain and drink accompaniments — is delivered to Israel complete and accurate. The completeness of the calendar's transmission is the OT image of the completeness of Christ's revelation: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work' (2 Timothy 3:16–17).
